78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
153

11	# Vanishing of `H¹` for skyscraper sheaves on a curve (RR.2.H¹)
12	
13	This file is the **RR.2.H¹** project-side build of the closed-point
14	skyscraper `H¹`-vanishing identity
15	
16	  `dim_{k̄} H¹(C, skyscraperSheaf P (ModuleCat.of k̄ k̄)) = 0`
17	
18	on a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / k̄`. The file is
19	the iter-191 Lane H **file-skeleton**: each of the eight pinned
20	declarations carries the *intended* substantive type signature (matching
21	the blueprint `\lean{...}` pin in `chapters/RiemannRoch_H1Vanishing.tex`),
22	with `sorry` bodies; the iter-192+ closure follows the classical
23	Hartshorne III.2.5 flasque-vanishing argument.
24	
25	The chapter strategy (Hartshorne III §2): an injective resolution of a
26	flasque sheaf yields a flasque quotient at each stage; the
27	global-sections functor is exact on a flasque-to-flasque-to-flasque
28	short exact sequence; hence the right-derived global-sections functor
29	vanishes on a flasque input in positive degree. We specialise the
30	abstract flasque-vanishing to the closed-point skyscraper sheaf, which
31	is flasque because it is the pushforward, along the closed embedding of
32	a one-point subspace, of the constant sheaf on an irreducible base.
33	
34	## Eight pinned declarations
35	
36	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque` — predicate on
37	   `Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)`.
38	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.pushforward` — pushforward of a
39	   flasque sheaf is flasque (Hartshorne II.1, Ex. 1.16(d)).
40	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.constant_of_irreducible` —
41	   constant sheaf on an irreducible space is flasque (Hartshorne
42	   II.1, Ex. 1.16(a)).
43	4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule_flasque_eq_zero` — flasque sheaves
44	   have zero `HModule` in positive degree (Hartshorne III.2.5).
45	5. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward_const` —
46	   skyscraper sheaf is the pushforward of a constant sheaf along the
47	   closed embedding of the closure of the support point.
48	6. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.closure_isIrreducible` — the
49	   closure of the support point of a `PrimeDivisor` is irreducible.
50	7. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque` —
51	   closed-point skyscraper sheaf is flasque.
52	8. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero` — the
53	   `RR.2.H¹` headline (`dim_{k̄} H¹(C, k(P)) = 0`), obtained by composing

80	/-! ## §1. Flasque sheaves of `kbar`-modules -/
81	
82	/-- **Flasque sheaf of `kbar`-modules on a topological space**
83	(Hartshorne II.1, Exercise 1.16; III.2, paragraph preceding Lemma 2.4).
84	
85	A sheaf `F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)`
86	is **flasque** when, for every inclusion of opens `V ≤ U` in `X`, the
87	restriction map
88	`F.val.obj (op U) → F.val.obj (op V)` is surjective as a map of
89	`kbar`-modules.
90	
91	The predicate is read off the underlying presheaf of `kbar`-modules and
92	applies uniformly to the project's `ModuleCat kbar`-flavoured cohomology
93	pipeline (it does not depend on any topological-space hypothesis on `X`,
94	so the curve specialisation is by instance synthesis on
95	`C.left.toTopCat`).
96	
97	Blueprint reference: `def:isFlasque_sheaf`. -/
98	def Scheme.IsFlasque
99	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}}
100	    (F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)) : Prop :=
101	  ∀ ⦃U V : TopologicalSpace.Opens X⦄ (h : V ≤ U),
102	    Function.Surjective ((F.val.map (homOfLE h).op).hom)
103	
104	/-- **Pushforward of a flasque sheaf is flasque** (Hartshorne II.1,
105	Exercise 1.16(d)).
106	
107	For a continuous map of topological spaces `f : X ⟶ Y` and a flasque
108	sheaf `F` of `kbar`-modules on `X`, the pushforward sheaf `f _* F` is
109	flasque on `Y`. The reason is purely formal: the restriction map of
110	`f _* F` along `V ≤ U` in `Y` is, by definition of pushforward, the
111	restriction map of `F` along `f ⁻¹ V ≤ f ⁻¹ U` in `X`, which is
112	surjective by hypothesis on `F`.
113	
114	**iter-191 Lane H prover dispatch** — closed via the unfolding
115	`pushforward.map (homOfLE h).op = F.val.map ((Opens.map f).map (homOfLE h)).op`
116	(by `rfl`), so flasqueness of `F` along `(Opens.map f).map (homOfLE h)` gives
117	the conclusion. Blueprint reference: `lem:isFlasque_pushforward`. -/
118	theorem Scheme.IsFlasque.pushforward
119	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X Y : TopCat.{u}} (f : X ⟶ Y)
120	    {F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)}
121	    (hF : Scheme.IsFlasque F) :
122	    Scheme.IsFlasque
123	      ((TopCat.Sheaf.pushforward (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) f).obj F) := by
124	  intro U V h
125	  exact hF ((Opens.map f).map (homOfLE h)).le
126	
127	/-- **Constant sheaf on an irreducible topological space is flasque**
128	(Hartshorne II.1, Exercise 1.16(a)).
129	
130	For an irreducible topological space `X`, every nonempty open is
131	connected and dense; the constant presheaf `U ↦ A` already satisfies the
132	sheaf condition, and its restriction maps are the identity on `A` (for
133	the nonempty branches) and factor through `0` (for the empty branch).
134	
135	**iter-191 Lane H file-skeleton** — Tier-3 honest typed sorry; closure
136	is iter-192+. Blueprint reference:
137	`lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible`. -/
138	theorem Scheme.IsFlasque.constant_of_irreducible
139	    (kbar : Type u) [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}}
140	    [IrreducibleSpace X] (A : ModuleCat.{u} kbar) :
141	    Scheme.IsFlasque
142	      ((constantSheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
143	        (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)).obj A) := by
144	  -- iter-196 Lane H prover attempt: case analysis on `V = ⊥`.
145	  -- Empty branch (closed below): `F.val.obj (op ⊥)` is terminal in `ModuleCat kbar`
146	  -- by `TopCat.Sheaf.isTerminalOfEqEmpty`; hence the underlying type is
147	  -- `Subsingleton`, so any element of the codomain equals the image of `0`.
148	  -- Non-empty branch (residual): on an irreducible space, every non-empty open is
149	  -- connected/dense, so the sheafification unit
150	  -- `A → ((constantSheaf J D).obj A).val.obj (op V)` is iso for non-empty `V`;
151	  -- composition with the inverse on `V` and the unit on `U` lifts any
152	  -- `y ∈ F(V)` to a preimage in `F(U)`. Formalising this requires the
153	  -- plus-construction characterisation of sheafification on irreducible spaces
154	  -- (Mathlib snapshot `b80f227` does not ship a direct lemma).
155	  intro U V h y
156	  by_cases hV : V = ⊥
157	  · -- empty branch: codomain is terminal, hence Subsingleton.
158	    subst hV
159	    have hterm : Limits.IsTerminal
160	        (((constantSheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
161	            (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)).obj A).val.obj
162	          (Opposite.op (⊥ : TopologicalSpace.Opens X))) :=
163	      TopCat.Sheaf.isTerminalOfEqEmpty
164	        ((constantSheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
165	          (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)).obj A) rfl
166	    have hzero : Limits.IsZero
167	        (((constantSheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
168	            (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)).obj A).val.obj
169	          (Opposite.op (⊥ : TopologicalSpace.Opens X))) :=
170	      hterm.isZero
171	    have hsub : Subsingleton
172	        ((((constantSheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
173	            (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)).obj A).val.obj
174	          (Opposite.op (⊥ : TopologicalSpace.Opens X))) : Type u) :=
175	      ModuleCat.subsingleton_of_isZero hzero
176	    exact ⟨0, Subsingleton.elim _ _⟩
177	  · -- non-empty branch: requires sheafification-unit-iso on irreducible space.
178	    sorry

180	/-- **Injective sheaves have vanishing higher cohomology** (axiom-clean
181	helper, Hartshorne III §1).
182	
183	For a topological space `X` and an injective object `I` of the
184	`kbar`-module sheaf category, the `kbar`-flavoured derived
185	global-sections cohomology `HModule kbar I i` is the zero `kbar`-module
186	for every `i ≥ 1`. This is immediate from Mathlib's
187	`HasInjectiveDimensionLT` framework: `Injective I` gives
188	`HasInjectiveDimensionLT I 1` (Mathlib
189	`instHasInjectiveDimensionLTOfNatNatOfInjective`), and
190	`HasInjectiveDimensionLT.subsingleton` yields
191	`Subsingleton (Abelian.Ext Y I i)` for `i ≥ 1` and any `Y`; specialising
192	`Y` to the constant sheaf at `kbar` and applying
193	`Module.finrank_zero_of_subsingleton` closes the goal.
194	
195	**iter-192 Lane H prover dispatch** — closed axiom-clean. Used as the
196	`Ext^i(_, I) = 0` input inside the Hartshorne III.2.5 long-exact-sequence
197	chase for `HModule_flasque_eq_zero` below. -/
198	theorem Scheme.HModule_injective_finrank_eq_zero
199	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}}
200	    [HasSheafify (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)]
201	    [HasExt (Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar))]
202	    {I : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)}
203	    [Injective I] (i : ℕ) (hi : 1 ≤ i) :
204	    Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar I i) = 0 := by
205	  have hsub : Subsingleton (Scheme.HModule kbar I i) :=
206	    HasInjectiveDimensionLT.subsingleton I 1 i hi _
207	  exact Module.finrank_zero_of_subsingleton
208	
209	/-- **The canonical injective-embedding short exact sequence**
210	`0 → F → Injective.under F → cokernel(Injective.ι F) → 0` of sheaves of
211	`kbar`-modules (axiom-clean helper). The embedding `Injective.ι F` is
212	mono by `Injective.ι_mono`, the cokernel projection is epi by
213	construction, and the middle row is exact at `Injective.under F` by
214	`ShortComplex.exact_cokernel`. -/
215	noncomputable def Scheme.injectiveSES
216	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}}
217	    [HasSheafify (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)]
218	    [HasExt (Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar))]
219	    (F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)) :
220	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex
221	      (Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)) :=
222	  ShortComplex.mk (Injective.ι F) (Limits.cokernel.π (Injective.ι F))
223	    (Limits.cokernel.condition (Injective.ι F))
224	
225	/-- The injective-embedding short complex is short exact. -/
226	theorem Scheme.injectiveSES_shortExact
227	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}}
228	    [HasSheafify (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)]
229	    [HasExt (Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar))]
230	    (F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)) :
231	    (Scheme.injectiveSES F).ShortExact :=
232	  { exact := ShortComplex.exact_cokernel (Injective.ι F)
233	    mono_f := Injective.ι_mono F
234	    epi_g := Limits.coequalizer.π_epi }
235	
236	/-- **Generic LES vanishing lemma**: in an abelian category with enough Ext,
237	if `0 → S.X₁ → S.X₂ → S.X₃ → 0` is short exact with `S.X₂` injective and the
238	post-composition `Hom(X, S.X₂) → Hom(X, S.X₃)` is surjective, then
239	`Ext X S.X₁ 1` is the zero `Subsingleton`.
240	
241	This is the structural skeleton of the Hartshorne III.2.5 argument
242	specialised at degree 1: the long exact sequence
243	`Ext X S.X₂ 0 → Ext X S.X₃ 0 → Ext X S.X₁ 1 → Ext X S.X₂ 1`
244	collapses because the rightmost term vanishes (injectivity of `S.X₂`)
245	and the leftmost map is surjective by the `Hom`-level hypothesis. The
246	proof composes:
247	- `HasInjectiveDimensionLT.subsingleton` (injective ⇒ `Ext X S.X₂ 1 = 0`).
248	- `Abelian.Ext.covariant_sequence_exact₁` (any `x₁ ∈ Ext X S.X₁ 1` lifts
249	  to some `x₃ ∈ Ext X S.X₃ 0` via the connecting morphism).
250	- `Abelian.Ext.addEquiv₀` (identifies `Ext X S.X₃ 0` with `Hom(X, S.X₃)`).
251	- `comp_extClass_assoc` (the LES "complex" identity used to discharge
252	  `(Ext.mk₀ S.g).comp extClass = 0`).
253	
254	**iter-192 Lane H prover dispatch** — closed axiom-clean. Used to peel
255	off the `i = 1` case of `HModule_flasque_eq_zero` once the
256	`Hom(X, I) → Hom(X, G)`-surjectivity input (Hartshorne II Ex. 1.16(b))
257	is supplied for an injective resolution. -/
258	theorem ext_one_eq_zero_of_hom_surjective_of_injective
259	    {C : Type v} [Category.{u} C] [Abelian C] [HasExt C]
260	    (X : C) {S : ShortComplex C} (hS : S.ShortExact) [Injective S.X₂]
261	    (hsurj : Function.Surjective (fun (f : X ⟶ S.X₂) => f ≫ S.g))
262	    (x₁ : Abelian.Ext X S.X₁ 1) : x₁ = 0 := by
263	  have hinj_subs : Subsingleton (Abelian.Ext X S.X₂ 1) :=
264	    HasInjectiveDimensionLT.subsingleton S.X₂ 1 1 le_rfl _
265	  have hker : x₁.comp (Abelian.Ext.mk₀ S.f) (show (1 : ℕ) + 0 = 1 by omega) = 0 :=
266	    Subsingleton.elim _ _
267	  obtain ⟨x₃, hx₃⟩ :=
268	    Abelian.Ext.covariant_sequence_exact₁ X hS x₁ hker
269	      (n₀ := 0) (show 0 + 1 = 1 by omega)
270	  obtain ⟨y, hy⟩ := hsurj (Abelian.Ext.addEquiv₀ x₃)
271	  simp only at hy
272	  have hx3eq : Abelian.Ext.mk₀ (Abelian.Ext.addEquiv₀ x₃) = x₃ := by
273	    rw [← Abelian.Ext.addEquiv₀_symm_apply, AddEquiv.symm_apply_apply]
274	  have hx3_factored :
275	      x₃ = (Abelian.Ext.mk₀ y).comp (Abelian.Ext.mk₀ S.g)
276	        (show (0 : ℕ) + 0 = 0 by omega) := by
277	    rw [Abelian.Ext.mk₀_comp_mk₀, hy, hx3eq]
278	  rw [← hx₃, hx3_factored]
279	  rw [Abelian.Ext.comp_assoc (Abelian.Ext.mk₀ y) (Abelian.Ext.mk₀ S.g) hS.extClass
280	      (show (0 : ℕ) + 0 = 0 by omega) (show (0 : ℕ) + 1 = 1 by omega)
281	      (show (0 : ℕ) + 0 + 1 = 1 by omega)]
282	  have hSg_extClass_zero :
283	      (Abelian.Ext.mk₀ S.g).comp hS.extClass (show (0 : ℕ) + 1 = 1 by omega) = 0 := by
284	    have key := hS.comp_extClass_assoc (Y := S.X₁) (n := 0)
285	      (Abelian.Ext.mk₀ (𝟙 S.X₁)) (n' := 1) (h := show (1 : ℕ) + 0 = 1 by omega)
286	    rw [Abelian.Ext.comp_mk₀_id] at key
287	    exact key
288	  rw [hSg_extClass_zero, Abelian.Ext.comp_zero]
289	
290	/-- **Higher-degree LES vanishing lemma** (axiom-clean structural helper,
291	iter-193 Lane H prover dispatch).
292	
293	Given a short exact sequence `0 → S.X₁ → S.X₂ → S.X₃ → 0` in an abelian
294	category with injective `S.X₂` and `n₀ ≥ 1`, if every element of
295	`Abelian.Ext X S.X₃ n₀` is zero, then so is every element of
296	`Abelian.Ext X S.X₁ (n₀ + 1)`. This is the higher-degree analogue of
297	`ext_one_eq_zero_of_hom_surjective_of_injective`: at degrees `n₀ ≥ 1` the

303	subsingleton, so the LES bracket `Ext X S.X₃ n₀ →ᵟ Ext X S.X₁ (n₀+1) →

308	    {C : Type v} [Category.{u} C] [Abelian C] [HasExt C]
309	    (X : C) {S : ShortComplex C} (hS : S.ShortExact) [Injective S.X₂]
310	    {n₀ : ℕ} (h_n₀ : 1 ≤ n₀)
311	    (hX₃ : ∀ y : Abelian.Ext X S.X₃ n₀, y = 0)

313	  have hinj_subs : Subsingleton (Abelian.Ext X S.X₂ (n₀ + 1)) :=
314	    HasInjectiveDimensionLT.subsingleton S.X₂ 1 (n₀ + 1) (by omega) _

316	      (show (n₀ + 1) + 0 = (n₀ + 1) by omega) = 0 :=
317	    Subsingleton.elim _ _

319	    Abelian.Ext.covariant_sequence_exact₁ X hS x₁ hker (rfl : n₀ + 1 = n₀ + 1)

438	/-- **Hartshorne II.1, Exercise 1.16(b)** (sections form): the
439	sheaf-morphism `S.g` is sectionwise-surjective on a short exact sequence
440	whose leftmost sheaf is flasque.
441	
442	For a sheaf-level short exact sequence
443	`0 → S.X₁ → S.X₂ → S.X₃ → 0` in `Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X)
444	(ModuleCat kbar)` with `S.X₁` flasque, the section-level map
445	`(S.g.val.app (op U)).hom : S.X₂.val.obj (op U) → S.X₃.val.obj (op U)`
446	is surjective for every open `U`.
447	
448	**Proof sketch** (Hartshorne II.1 Ex 1.16(b), Zorn's lemma):
449	Let `t ∈ S.X₃.val.obj (op U)`. Consider pairs `(V, s)` where `V ⊆ U` is
450	open and `s ∈ S.X₂.val.obj (op V)` restricts to `t|_V`. Order by
451	extension. Any chain has an upper bound (sheaf gluing condition on
452	`S.X₂`). By Zorn's lemma, a maximal element `(V₀, s₀)` exists. Suppose
453	`V₀ ≠ U`. Pick `x ∈ U \ V₀`. Stalkwise, `S.X₂_x → S.X₃_x` is surjective
454	(SES in the abelian sheaf category preserves stalks). So in some
455	neighborhood `W` of `x` (after shrinking), there is
456	`s'_W ∈ S.X₂.val.obj (op W)` with image `t|_W`. On `V₀ ∩ W`,
457	`s₀ - s'_W` lies in `S.X₁.val.obj (op (V₀ ∩ W))` (since both map to
458	`t|_{V₀∩W}`). Use flasqueness of `S.X₁` to extend the difference to
459	`α ∈ S.X₁.val.obj (op W)`. Set `s'' := s'_W + α`. Then `s''` agrees with
460	`s₀` on `V₀ ∩ W`. By sheaf gluing on `S.X₂`, get a unique
461	`s''' ∈ S.X₂.val.obj (op (V₀ ∪ W))` extending both. This contradicts
462	maximality. Hence `V₀ = U`.

731	III.2, Proposition 2.5).
732	
733	For a topological space `X` and a flasque sheaf `F` of `kbar`-modules on
734	`X`, the `kbar`-flavoured derived global-sections cohomology
735	`HModule kbar F i` is the zero `kbar`-module for every `i ≥ 1`. In
736	particular, `dim_{kbar} HModule kbar F 1 = 0`.

738	The proof structure mirrors Hartshorne III §2 verbatim: embed `F` into
739	an injective `I` of the abelian sheaf category, form the quotient short
740	exact sequence `0 → F → I → G → 0` (Mathlib: `Scheme.injectiveSES`,
741	axiom-clean), observe that `G` inherits flasqueness from `F` and `I`
742	(the latter by Hartshorne III Lemma 2.4), read off the short exact
743	sequence on global sections from the flasque input
744	(Hartshorne II Ex. 1.16(b)), and apply the long exact sequence
745	(`Abelian.Ext.covariant_sequence_exact₁`) to get `HModule kbar F 1 = 0`
746	and a reduction `HModule kbar F i ≅ HModule kbar G (i - 1)` for `i ≥ 2`;
747	iteration closes the higher-degree cases.

750	sorries are isolated to two named substrate helpers

3	\chapter{Vanishing of \(H^1\) for skyscraper sheaves on a curve (RR.2.H\(^1\))}
4	\label{chap:RR_H1Vanishing}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.1, Exercise~1.16
7	% (flasque sheaves), Exercise~1.17 (skyscraper sheaves); III.2,
8	% Proposition~2.5 (flasque sheaves are acyclic) (read from
9	% references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 67--68 and
10	% 225). All verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks below were
11	% copied character-by-character from the rendered page images of the
12	% scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
13	
14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	
16	This chapter is the project-side build of the
17	\texttt{RR.2.H\(^1\)} sub-phase, committed in iter-188 of the strategy
18	revision: \emph{the first cohomology of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf
19	on a smooth proper curve vanishes}. Concretely, the chapter pins the
20	identity
21	\[
22	  \dim_{\bar k}\, H^1\bigl(C,\, \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
23	    (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)\bigr) \;=\; 0
24	\]
25	on a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\), where
26	\(P \in C\) is a closed point and the cohomology is computed through the
27	project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured \(\Ext\)-cohomology pipeline
28	\(\HModule \bar k\) (chapter~REF, sibling
29	\texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex}).
30	
31	\paragraph{Why a dedicated chapter.} Mathlib (snapshot
32	\texttt{b80f227}) does \emph{not} ship a flasque-cohomology vanishing
33	lemma at the \(\Ext\)-cohomology level used by
34	\(\HModule \bar k\), nor a Serre-duality theorem for curves. The
35	genus-\(0\) critical path nevertheless needs the skyscraper
36	\(H^1\)-vanishing twice: once inside the Hartshorne~IV.1.3
37	\(\chi\)-additivity assembly
38	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}, the parent lemma
39	\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_skyscraperSheaf}), and once inside the
40	\texttt{RR.3} chain that produces a non-constant section
41	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}, the downstream consumer
42	\texttt{h1\_vanishing\_genusZero}). The present chapter unifies the
43	substrate behind both consumers into a single short, self-contained
44	project-side build.
45	
46	\paragraph{Strategy of the chapter.} The argument is the classical
47	Hartshorne~III~\S2 one: an injective resolution of a flasque sheaf
48	yields a flasque quotient at each stage (since both the injective object
49	and the original sheaf are flasque), and the global-sections functor is
50	exact on a short exact sequence of flasque sheaves. Iterating, every
51	derived functor of \(\Gamma\) vanishes on a flasque input in positive
52	degree. The chapter then specialises to the closed-point skyscraper
53	sheaf, which is flasque by inspection (it is the pushforward, along the
54	inclusion of a one-point closed subspace, of the constant sheaf on an
55	irreducible base — and pushforwards of flasque sheaves are flasque).
56	
57	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is
58	an algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
59	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). The Lean signatures (planned location
60	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/H1Vanishing.lean}) carry the same
61	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API
62	(\(\mathtt{IsProper}\), \(\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\, 1\),
63	\(\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\), \(\mathtt{IsIntegral}\)). The
64	flasque-vanishing lemma of \S2 below is stated more generally for a
65	noetherian topological space (the natural generality of
66	Hartshorne~III.2.5); the curve specialisation is read off by instance
67	synthesis on the underlying space \(C.\mathtt{left.toTopCat}\).
68	
69	\section{Flasque sheaves and their cohomology vanishing}
70	\label{sec:H1Vanishing_flasque}

100	  applied to a sheaf
101	  \(\mathcal F : \mathrm{Sheaf}\;J\;(\Module \bar k)\), where \(J\) is the
102	  Grothendieck topology of opens on the underlying topological space of
103	  the curve \(C\). The surjectivity condition is read on the underlying
104	  presheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules; the project predicate
105	  \texttt{Scheme.IsFlasque} packages exactly this condition.
106	
107	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean predicate
108	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque} takes a sheaf
109	  \(\mathcal F : \mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
110	  (\Module \bar k)\) and asserts that for every pair \(V \le U\) of opens
111	  of \(X\), the restriction morphism
112	  \((\mathcal F.\mathtt{val.map}\;(\mathrm{leOp}\;V\;U)) :
113	  \mathcal F.\mathtt{val.obj}\;(\mathrm{op}\;U) \to
114	  \mathcal F.\mathtt{val.obj}\;(\mathrm{op}\;V)\) is surjective as a map of
115	  \(\bar k\)-modules.

118	\begin{lemma}
119	
120	\leanok
121	[Pushforward of a flasque sheaf is flasque]
122	  \label{lem:isFlasque_pushforward}
123	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.pushforward}
124	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
125	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.16(d) (read from
126	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 67).
127	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(f: X \to Y\) is a continuous map, and if
128	  % \(\mathscr{F}\) is a flasque sheaf on \(X\), then \(f_*\mathscr{F}\)
129	  % is a flasque sheaf on \(Y\)."
130	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.16(d).}
131	  Let \(f \colon X \to Y\) be a continuous map of topological spaces and
132	  let \(\mathcal F\) be a flasque sheaf on \(X\). Then the pushforward
133	  sheaf \(f_*\mathcal F\) is flasque on \(Y\).
134	\end{lemma}
135	
136	\begin{proof}
137	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
138	  \leanok
139	  For an inclusion of opens \(V \subseteq U\) in \(Y\), the restriction
140	  map of \(f_*\mathcal F\) is by definition the restriction map of
141	  \(\mathcal F\) on the open sets \(f^{-1}(V) \subseteq f^{-1}(U)\) of
142	  \(X\):
143	  \[
144	    (f_*\mathcal F)(U) \;=\; \mathcal F(f^{-1}(U))
145	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\mathcal F(f^{-1}(V) \subseteq f^{-1}(U))\;}\;
146	    \mathcal F(f^{-1}(V)) \;=\; (f_*\mathcal F)(V).
147	  \]
148	  Since \(\mathcal F\) is flasque on \(X\), the right-hand restriction is
149	  surjective, hence so is the left-hand one. Therefore \(f_*\mathcal F\)
150	  is flasque on \(Y\).
151	\end{proof}

393	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. Embed \(\mathscr{F}\) in an injective
394	% object \(\mathscr{I}\) of \(\mathfrak{Ab}(X)\) and let \(\mathscr{G}\)
395	% be the quotient:
396	% \(0 \to \mathscr{F} \to \mathscr{I} \to \mathscr{G} \to 0\).
397	% Then \(\mathscr{F}\) is flasque by hypothesis, \(\mathscr{I}\) is
398	% flasque by (2.4), and so \(\mathscr{G}\) is flasque by (II, Ex. 1.16c).

400	% Ex. 1.16b)
401	% \(0 \to \Gamma(X, \mathscr{F}) \to \Gamma(X, \mathscr{I}) \to

404	% \(H^i(X, \mathscr{I}) = 0\) for \(i > 0\) (1.1Ae). Thus from the long
405	% exact sequence of cohomology, we get \(H^1(X, \mathscr{F}) = 0\) and
406	% \(H^i(X, \mathscr{F}) \cong H^{i-1}(X, \mathscr{G})\) for each
407	% \(i \geq 2\). But \(\mathscr{G}\) is also flasque, so by induction on
408	% \(i\) we get the result."
409	\begin{proof}
410	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
411	  \leanok
412	  We follow Hartshorne's induction. Embed \(\mathcal F\) into an
413	  injective object \(\mathcal I\) of the abelian category
414	  \(\mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
415	  (\Module \bar k)\) — such an injective exists by the
416	  enough-injectives instance for sheaves of \(\bar k\)-modules on opens
417	  (\cref{chap:Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK}). Form the quotient
418	  short exact sequence
419	  \[
420	    0 \to \mathcal F \to \mathcal I \to \mathcal G \to 0
421	  \]
422	  in the sheaf category.
423	
424	  \emph{Step 1: the quotient \(\mathcal G\) is flasque.} The hypothesis
425	  gives that \(\mathcal F\) is flasque. The injective object
426	  \(\mathcal I\) is flasque by the Hartshorne~III~Lemma~2.4 input:
427	  any injective \(\mathcal O_X\)-module is flasque, where the
428	  \(\mathcal O_X\) here is the constant sheaf of rings \(\bar k\) on
429	  \(X\), so the sheaf category over the constant sheaf of rings is the
430	  category of sheaves of \(\bar k\)-modules and the lemma applies. From
431	  the two flasqueness inputs the quotient \(\mathcal G\) inherits
432	  flasqueness by Hartshorne~II~Exercise~1.16(c): \emph{if
433	  \(0 \to \mathcal F' \to \mathcal F \to \mathcal F'' \to 0\) is an
434	  exact sequence and \(\mathcal F', \mathcal F\) are flasque, then
435	  \(\mathcal F''\) is flasque}.
436	
437	  \emph{Step 2: the global-sections functor is exact on the chosen
438	  short exact sequence.} Since the leftmost term \(\mathcal F\) is
439	  flasque, the long exact sequence of cohomology starts with a short
440	  exact sequence on global sections
441	  (Hartshorne~II~Exercise~1.16(b)):
442	  \[
443	    0 \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal F) \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal I) \to
444	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal G) \to 0.
445	  \]
446	
447	  \emph{Step 3: the long exact sequence gives the vanishing.}
448	  The object \(\mathcal I\) is injective in the abelian sheaf category,
449	  hence its right-derived global-sections cohomology vanishes in
450	  positive degree:
451	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal I\;i = 0\) for all \(i \ge 1\). The long
452	  exact sequence of the short exact sequence above, applied to the
453	  derived functor \(\HModule\;\bar k\;{-}\;{\bullet}\) of \(\Gamma\),
454	  reads in degrees \(0\) and \(1\) as
455	  \[
456	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal I) \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal G)
457	    \to \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1
458	    \to \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal I\;1 = 0.
459	  \]
460	  The leftmost map is surjective by Step~2, so the connecting morphism
461	  to \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1\) is the zero map, and the kernel
462	  of \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 \to 0\) is all of
463	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1\); hence
464	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 = 0\).
465	
466	  \emph{Step 4: higher degrees by induction.} For \(i \ge 2\), the long
467	  exact sequence yields
468	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;i \cong \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal G\;
469	  (i - 1)\); the inductive hypothesis on the flasque quotient
470	  \(\mathcal G\) gives the right-hand side is zero. This closes the
471	  vanishing in all positive degrees. The chapter consumes only the
472	  degree-\(1\) instance, but the lemma is stated and proved in full
473	  generality so it can support later sub-builds (e.g.\ Grothendieck
474	  vanishing on a one-dimensional scheme).
